revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:葉室古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Hamuro Kofun Group, gaikan.jpg|location={{ill|Taishi|qid=Q1347173|1=Taishi, Osaka}}|part_of={{ill|Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11588120|1=Shinagadani Kofun Cluster}}}} [[File:Hamuro Kofun Group, gaikan.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Kamadozuka Kofun (right), Ishizuka Kofun (center right),
and Hamurozuka Kofun (left back)}}]] {{Location map|Japan Osaka|lat_deg=34|lat_min=30|lat_sec=40.17|lon_deg=135|lon_min=38|lon_sec=36.33|label=Hamuro Kofun Group|position=top|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{center|Location of the Hamuro Kofun Group}}}} {{OSM Location map | coord = {{Coord|34|30|58|N|135|38|43|E}} | zoom = 14 | float = right | width = 320| height =250 | caption = {{center|Distribution map of {{ill|Isonokami Kofun Cluster|ja|磯長谷古墳群|1=Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonokami Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|qq=}}}} | scalemark = 60 | mark-coord1= {{Coord|34|30|42.60|N|135|38|7.75|E}} | label1=Taishi Nishiyama (Emperor Bidatsu) | label-pos1=top | label-color1=black | mark1=前方後円墳-北西.png | mark-size1=30 | mark-coord2= {{Coord|34|31|0.43|N|135|38|39.13|E}} | label2=Kasuga Mukoyama (Emperor Yōmei) | label-pos2=top | label-color2=black | mark2=方墳.png | mark-size2=30 | mark-coord3= {{Coord|34|30|42.83|N|135|38|58.44|E}} | label3=Yamada Takatsuka (Empress Suiko) | label-pos3=top | label-color3=black | mark3=方墳.png | mark-size3=30 | mark-coord4= {{Coord|34|31|3.14|N|135|39|15.84|E}} | label4=Yamada Uenoyama (Emperor Kōtoku) | label-pos4=top | label-color4=black | mark4=円墳.png | mark-size4=30 | mark-coord5= {{Coord|34|31|11.23|N|135|38|21.38|E}} | label5=Eifukuji North (Prince Shōtoku) | label-pos5=top | label-color5=black | mark5=円墳.png | mark-size5=30 | mark-coord6= {{Coord|34|30|38.96|N|135|39|5.65|E}} | label6=Futagozuka | label-pos6=bottom | label-color6=black | mark6=方墳.png | mark-size6=30 | mark-coord7= {{Coord|34|30|41.09|N|135|38|32.64|E}} | label7=Kamadozuka | label-pos7=top | label-color7=black | mark7=円墳.png | mark-size7=30 | mark-coord8= {{Coord|34|30|38.94|N|135|38|33.84|E}} | label8=Ishizuka | label-pos8=bottom | label-color8=black | mark8=円墳.png | mark-size8=30 | mark-coord9= {{Coord|34|30|40.17|N|135|38|36.33|E}} | label9=Hamurozuka | label-pos9=right | label-color9=black | mark9=方墳.png | mark-size9=30 }} '''Hamuro Kofun Group''' (葉室古墳群) is a group of kofun (ancient Kofuns) located in {{ill|Taishi, Osaka|en|Taishi, Osaka|qid=Q1347173|lt=Taishi, Osaka|1=Taishi, Osaka}}, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Isonokami Tani Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群|1=Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonokami Tani Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|qq=}} but has not been designated as a historic site. == Overview == Located in southeastern Osaka Prefecture, the Hamuro Kofun Group is situated along the ridgeline stretching east-west between the southwest of the Yamada settlement and the east of the Hamuro settlement near the foothills of {{ill|Mount Nijō|en|Mount Nijō|qid=Q11371271|lt=Mount Nijō|1=Mount Nijō}}. It dates to the late Kofun period {{Sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Valley of Royal Mausolea and Isonokami Tani Kofun Group|1994|pp=33-41}}. Key members of this group include Kamadozuka Kofun, Ishizuka Kofun, Hamurozuka Kofun (also known as Echizen-zuka Kofun), and Mondozuka Kofun. A detailed survey was conducted in 1972 {{Sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Valley of Royal Mausolea and Isonokami Tani Kofun Group|1994|pp=33-41}}. This kofun group is notable for features such as the adoption of {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|en|Iwayayama Kofun|qid=Q11474389|lt=Iwayayama-style stone chambers|1=Iwayayama Kofun}} associated with imperial and aristocratic burials. The mound size of Hamurozuka Kofun rivals that of contemporaneous imperial mausolea, showcasing its prominence {{Sfn|Valley of Royal Mausolea and Isonokami Tani Kofun Group|1994|pp=33-41}}. The Kofuns were oriented with an awareness of views from the south, demonstrating a strong connection to the nearby {{ill|Ichisuka Kofun Cluster|en|Ichisuka Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11353283|lt=Ichisuga Kofun Group|1=Ichisuka Kofun Cluster}} {{Sfn|Valley of Royal Mausolea and Isonokami Tani Kofun Group|1994|pp=33-41}}. The spatial distribution of kofun along the Tai River shifted upstream over time, transitioning from the {{ill|Kyuryudani Kofun Group|ja|九流谷古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyuryudani Kofun Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504629|1=Kyuryudani Kofun Group}} to {{ill|Taishi Nishiyama Kofun|ja|太子西山古墳|1=Taishi Nishiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishi Nishiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishi Nishiyama Kofun|qq=}} (modern {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|en|Emperor Bidatsu|qid=Q316637|lt=Emperor Bidatsu|1=Emperor Bidatsu}}’s tomb) and then to the Hamuro Kofun Group {{Sfn|Valley of Royal Mausolea and Isonokami Tani Kofun Group|1994|pp=33-41}}. Given its characteristics and association with the rise of the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|qid=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} and their connections to immigrant populations, the Hamuro Kofun Group is thought to be deeply tied to the Soga clan's history {{Sfn|Valley of Royal Mausolea and Isonokami Tani Kofun Group|1994|pp=33-41}}. The group's proximity to other key kofun linked to emperors and nobles during the late Kofun and Asuka periods underscores its archaeological significance. == List == === Kamadozuka Kofun === [[File:Kamadozuka Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Kamadozuka Kofun}}{{small|Depression at the center indicating the remains of the stone chamber.}}]] '''Kamadozuka Kofun''' (釜戸塚古墳, {{coord|34|30|41.09|N|135|38|32.64|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Kamadozuka Kofun}}) is a [[circular Kofun (円墳)|circular Kofun]]{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The mound measures 45 meters in diameter and over 5 meters in height, though it suffered significant destruction early on (reports indicate it was already damaged during reclamation){{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. No {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} were detected on the mound's surface, but scattered stones presumed to be roof tiles were noted during the reclamation process{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. A horseshoe-shaped moat surrounds the mound{{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The burial facility is thought to have been a [[horizontal stone chamber]], and the characteristics of the remaining stones suggest it followed the Iwayayama-style chamber ([[Iwayayama Kofun]], [[Asuka Village]], [[Takaichi District]], {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}){{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. Construction is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|qid=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}{{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. Within the Isonokami Valley, only Kamadozuka Kofun and Hamurozuka Kofun feature moats outside of imperial tombs. Given Kamadozuka Kofun's comparable scale and chamber style to the {{ill|Eifukuji North Kofun|ja|叡福寺北古墳|1=Eifuku-ji North Kofun|2=ja|3=叡福寺北古墳|4=fr|5=Eifukuji Kita Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eifukuji North Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Eifuku-ji North Kofun|qq=}} (Prince Shōtoku's tomb), it is speculated to be the burial site of a powerful noble{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. Some theories link the name "Kamadozuka" to "Kikami Kamado no Musume" mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|qid=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}} (697 AD) and propose it as a tomb of the Kimi clan, though others argue that the name simply reflects the local use of soil for stove-making{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. === Ishizuka Kofun === [[File:Hamuro Ishizuka Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ishizuka Kofun}}]] '''Ishizuka Kofun''' (石塚古墳), also known as '''Hamuro Ishizuka Kofun''' (葉室石塚古墳, {{coord|34|30|38.94|N|135|38|33.84|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Hamuro Ishizuka Kofun}}), is a circular mound (previously theorized as a [[Square Kofun|Square mound]]){{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The mound measures 30 meters in diameter and 4 meters in height{{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. No haniwa were detected, but scattered stones presumed to be roof tiles were observed{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The burial facility was a horizontal stone chamber, now destroyed{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The chamber originally measured 10.6 meters in length, with the burial chamber measuring 6.1 meters in length, 2.1 meters in width, and 2.6 meters in height, while the passage was 1.7 meters wide and 1.8 meters high{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. Although similar to the Iwayayama-style chamber, it is considered an earlier form{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The burial chamber housed a house-shaped Sarcophagus, while the passage contained a wooden coffin{{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. Excavated items include two examples of {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|qid=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}}, specifically a crucible and a fragment of a long-necked jar{{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The construction is dated to the late {{ill|6th century|en|6th century|qid=Q8090|lt=6th century|1=6th century}} to early {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|qid=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. === Hamurozuka Kofun === [[File:Hamurozuka Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hamurozuka Kofun}}{{small|Left: western mound; Right rear: eastern mound.}}]] '''Hamurozuka Kofun''' (葉室塚古墳) or '''Koshimaezuka Kofun''' (越前塚古墳, {{coord|34|30|40.17|N|135|38|36.33|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Hamurozuka Kofun}}) is identified as a {{ill|Rectangular Kofun|ja|長方形墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (長方墳) or a pair of connected square mounds (双方墳), though its exact structure is uncertain{{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The mound was constructed in two tiers, measuring 75 meters east-to-west, 55 meters north-to-south, and over 8 meters in height, though the western half has suffered significant damage{{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. Parts of the moat remain visible{{sfn|Hamuro Kofun Group (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. Although the burial facilities are not confirmed, it is speculated that each half had a horizontal stone chamber opening southward{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. Construction is estimated to date to the early {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|qid=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. The form of Hamurozuka Kofun shares similarities with the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}} (the joint burial of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} and {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|竹田皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|1=Prince Takeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11599359}}) and the {{ill|Futagozuka Kofun (Osaka)|en|Futagozuka Kofun (Osaka)|qid=Q11371443|lt=Futagozuka Kofun (Osaka)|1=Futagozuka Kofun (Osaka)}}. The mound's dimensions and construction scale suggest it may have been intended for a figure of imperial Rank, such as [[Emperor Bidatsu]] or {{ill|Princess Ishi-hime|en|Princess Ishi-hime|qid=Q11585498|lt=Princess Ishi-hime|1=Princess Ishi-hime}}. However, its location within a cluster of other tombs has raised doubts about its use as an imperial tomb, with some suggesting it may belong to a noble family, particularly the [[Soga clan]]. The identity of the interred remains uncertain{{sfn|Valley of Royal Tombs and Isonokami Kofun Group|1994|pp=33–41}}. == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Isonokami Kofun Cluster|ja|磯長谷古墳群|1=Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isonokami Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagadani Kofun Cluster|qq=}} * {{ill|Ichisuka Kofun Cluster|en|Ichisuka Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11353283|lt=Ichisuga Kofun Group|1=Ichisuka Kofun Cluster}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|葉室古墳群|version=93995127|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:葉室古墳群]] {{Commons category|Hamuro Kofun Group}} {{wikidata link|Q48753076}} [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Kofun of Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal History of Taishi, Ōsaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Rectangular Kofun]] [[Category:Shinagadani Kofun Cluster]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
